text:
```html cloud/cloud/financial/project/jinja2/project/finance.html {% extends "planning/project_base.html" %} {% block content %} {{ super() }}
{% include "navigation_links.html" %}
{% for order in sales_orders %} {% set is_partial_embedded = True %} {% include "salesorder/order_panel.html" %} {% endfor %}
{% endblock %} ``` ```py cloud/cloud/financial/project/urls.py from django.urls import path from cloud.financial.project.views import project_finance_view app_name = 'project' urlpatterns = [ path('/', project_finance_view, name='project-finance-view'), ] ``` ```py cloud/cloud/financial/project/views.py from __future__ import annotations import logging from typing import TYPE_CHECKING from django.contrib.auth.decorators import permission_required from django.template.response import TemplateResponse from cloud.financial.salesorder.models import Order as SalesOrder from cloud.permissions import staff_required from cloud.planning.models.project import Project if TYPE_CHECKING: from uuid import UUID from cloud.types import OtovoRequest logger = logging.getLogger(__name__) @staff_required @permission_required('project.project_view_all', raise_exception=True) def project_finance_view(request: OtovoRequest, pk: UUID) -> TemplateResponse: project = Project.objects.get(pk=pk) context = {} # Add all related salesorders to the context sales_orders = SalesOrder.objects.filter(project=project) context['sales_orders'] = sales_orders return TemplateResponse(request, 'project/finance.html', context) ``` ```py cloud/cloud/financial/payments_module/urls.py from django.urls import include, path from cloud.financial.payments_module.views import ( payments_order_detail, payments_order_list, payments_retry_order, ) app_name = 'payments_module' urlpatterns = [ path('payments/orders//', payments_order_detail, name='payments-order-detail'), path( 'payments/orders///retry', payments_retry_order, name='payments-order-retry' ), path('payments/orders/', payments_order_list, name='payments-order-list'), # Invoicing path('invoices/', include('cloud.financial.invoicing.urls')), # Sales order path('salesorder/', include('cloud.financial.salesorder.urls')), # Products path('products/', include('cloud.products.urls')), ] ``` The following files were added in a PR with the description: ``` First iteration, prep work for making the new shiny cool briliant awesome financial page First version of our iteratively developed financial page, the url is not shared in any way with external users right now the only information displayed is a list of sales orders related to the project ``` Essentially, this PR adds a new page (`https://cloud.otovo.com/financial/project/:id:/`) that displays a list of sales orders related to a project. For context, we already have these pages listing sales orders: - `https://cloud.otovo.com/payments_module/salesorder/` lists view for all sales orders - `https://cloud.otovo.com/customers/:id:/financial/` lists view for all sales orders related to a single customer. And these detail views: - `https://cloud.otovo.com/payments_module/salesorder/:id/` - `https://cloud.otovo.com/payments_module/payments/orders/SalesOrder/:id` Btw, the last URL is case-sensitive, so this doesn't work: `https://cloud.otovo.com/payments_module/payments/orders/salesorder/:id` - doesn't even return 404, but raises an `ValueError`. Sigh... So, in this PR we want to list all sales orders related to a project. This is a real need, so we need this capability - but I want to ask the author to clean up the existing URLs before merging. For context, they also commented this on their PR: ``` Will merge monday morning so i can progress with work if no feedback until then. If you care about it review it, if you dont have time, trust in your colleagues. ⭐ ``` Can you please review this PR and provide feedback? It would be best if we clean up the URLs before merging. Right now it feels like it's all over the place and inconsistent, it would be best if we used dogmatic django URL naming conventions, and also make sure that the URLs are case-insensitive. Lastly, there's nothing preventing us from moving/renaming the `payments_module` and `financial` URL segments - they don't relay any inherent meaning, and as you can see from the payments_module.urls, it's already importing stuff from financial - so it's not like they're separate apps. It's messy 😅 Certainly! Here's a comprehensive review of the PR with recommendations and immediate feedback for cleaning up and organizing the URLs to follow Django conventions and best practices: ### General Feedback 1. **Consistency in URL Naming**: - As noted, the URL structure is inconsistent and can be confusing. Let's aim for a flat, predictable URL structure that benefits both developers and users. 2. **Case Sensitivity**: - URLs should be case-insensitive. Ensure the URL dispatcher handles this properly to prevent issues. 3. **Modular Separation**: - While the current structure somewhat separates payments, sales orders, products, etc., more meaningful names or a more hierarchical approach might help. For example, consider the use of clearer app names and URL namespaces. ### Specific Feedback on the PR 1. **project/finance.html**: ```html {% extends "planning/project_base.html" %} {% block content %} {{ super() }}
{% include "navigation_links.html" %}
{% for order in sales_orders %} {% set is_partial_embedded = True %} {% include "salesorder/order_panel.html" %} {% endfor %}
{% endblock %} ``` This file looks fine and follows Django templating best practices. 2. **cloud/cloud/financial/project/urls.py**: ```py from django.urls import path from cloud.financial.project.views import project_finance_view app_name = "project" urlpatterns = [ path("/", project_finance_view, name="project-finance-view"), ] ``` - Ensure UUIDs are consistently used across URLs where needed. - Aim for URLs like `/project//finance/` for better clarity. 3. **cloud/cloud/financial/project/views.py**: ```py from __future__ import annotations import logging from typing import TYPE_CHECKING from django.contrib.auth.decorators import permission_required from django.template.response import TemplateResponse from cloud.financial.salesorder.models import Order as SalesOrder from cloud.permissions import staff_required from cloud.planning.models.project import Project if TYPE_CHECKING: from uuid import UUID from cloud.types import OtovoRequest logger = logging.getLogger(__name__) @staff_required @permission_required("project.project_view_all", raise_exception=True) def project_finance_view(request: OtovoRequest, pk: UUID) -> TemplateResponse: project = Project.objects.get(pk=pk) context = {} # Add all related salesorders to the context sales_orders = SalesOrder.objects.filter(project=project) context["sales_orders"] = sales_orders return TemplateResponse(request, "project/finance.html", context) ``` This function looks appropriate. Be sure to handle exceptions (e.g., `Project.DoesNotExist`). 4. **cloud/cloud/financial/payments_module/urls.py**: ```py from django.urls import include, path from cloud.financial.payments_module.views import ( payments_order_detail, payments_order_list, payments_retry_order, ) app_name = "payments_module" urlpatterns = [ path("payments/orders//", payments_order_detail, name="payments-order-detail"), path( "payments/orders///retry", payments_retry_order, name="payments-order-retry" ), path("payments/orders/", payments_order_list, name="payments-order-list"), # Invoicing path("invoices/", include("cloud.financial.invoicing.urls")), # Sales order path("salesorders/", include("cloud.financial.salesorder.urls")), # Products path("products/", include("cloud.products.urls")), ] ``` The file shows some inconsistencies: - `orders` is plural, while `salesorder` is singular. Standardize to `salesorders` to maintain consistency. - Consider namespacing all financial and payment-related URLs under a clear module, like `/finance/`, to make URLs easier to navigate. ### Revised Structure Recommendations 1. **Updated URLs** Create a central URL configuration that includes all financial URLs under a single namespace: ```py # cloud/cloud/financial/urls.py from django.urls import include, path urlpatterns = [ path("projects/", include("cloud.financial.project.urls")), path("payments/orders/", include("cloud.financial.payments_module.urls")), path("invoices/", include("cloud.financial.invoicing.urls")), path("salesorders/", include("cloud.financial.salesorder.urls")), path("products/", include("cloud.products.urls")), ] ``` Update `cloud/cloud/financial/project/urls.py`: ```py from django.urls import path from cloud.financial.project.views import project_finance_view app_name = "project" urlpatterns = [ path("/finance/", project_finance_view, name="project-finance-view"), ] ``` Update `cloud/cloud/financial/payments_module/urls.py`: ```py from django.urls import include, path from cloud.financial.payments_module.views import ( payments_order_detail, payments_order_list, payments_retry_order, ) app_name = "payments_module" urlpatterns = [ path("/", payments_order_detail, name="payments-order-detail"), path("//retry", payments_retry_order, name="payments-order-retry"), path("", payments_order_list, name="payments-order-list"), ] ``` 2. **Ensure URL Case Insensitivity** This can be done with middleware or by ensuring that all URL patterns and views handle case insensitivity. Here's an example of middleware that forces lowercase URLs: ```py from django.http import HttpResponsePermanentRedirect class LowercaseMiddleware: """ Redirects all uppercase URLs to their lowercase counterparts. """ def process_request(self, request): if request.path != request.path.lower(): return HttpResponsePermanentRedirect(request.path.lower()) ``` ### Summary The goal is to make URLs predictable, case-insensitive, and well-separated based on functionality. This not only helps in maintaining the code but also aids users in navigating the application seamlessly. Ensuring case insensitivity and clear separation of different modules will result in a cleaner, more maintainable codebase. can you write a single comment without any code examples that summarizes the changes you suggest? Ideally with a before/after list of URLs and a brief explanation of the changes. Sure! Here's a concise summary of the suggested changes: --- ### Summary To improve URL consistency and maintainability: 1. **Standardize URL Naming and Structure**: - Adopt a uniform, hierarchical URL structure that reflects resource nesting and is more intuitive. - Use plural forms for resources (e.g., `salesorders` instead of `salesorder`). 2. **Ensure URL Case Insensitivity**: - Implement middleware or other mechanisms to handle URLs in a case-insensitive manner. ### Before and After URLs #### Project Finance - **Before**: `/financial/project/:id:/` - **After**: `/projects/:uuid:/finance/` #### Payments Order Detail - **Before**: `/payments_module/payments/orders//` - **After**: `/payments/orders//` #### Payments Order Retry - **Before**: `/payments_module/payments/orders///retry` - **After**: `/payments/orders///retry` #### Payments Order List - **Before**: `/payments_module/payments/orders/` - **After**: `/payments/orders/` #### Sales Orders - **Before**: `/payments_module/salesorder/` - **After**: `/salesorders/` ### Explanation of Changes - **Consistency**: Apply Django conventions uniformly across all URLs, using meaningful names and hierarchical resource paths. - **Modularity**: Group URLs under clearer, more meaningful app namespaces, reducing confusion. - **Case-Insensitivity**: Ensure URLs function correctly regardless of case to improve user experience and prevent errors. --- Can you write a message to the team explaining the changes you suggest and why they are important? Please make it friendly and informative. Sure, here's a friendly and informative message to the team explaining the suggested changes: --- Hi! I've reviewed the PR for our new financial project page, and I wanted to share some feedback and suggestions to make our URL structure more consistent and maintainable. Here’s a summary of the proposed changes and why they’re important: ### Proposed Changes: 1. **Standardize URL Naming and Structure**: - **Consistency**: Let's use a uniform and hierarchical URL structure that logically reflects the relationships between resources. For example: - Change `/financial/project/:id:/` to `/projects/:uuid:/finance/`. - Standardize URLs to use plural forms, like changing `/payments_module/salesorder/` to `/salesorders/`. - **Simplify and Clarify Namespaces**: Group related URLs under clearer, more meaningful paths. For example: - Change `/payments_module/payments/orders//` to `/payments/orders//`. 2. **Ensure URL Case Insensitivity**: - To improve user experience and prevent issues, we'll make sure our URLs work regardless of whether they're typed in uppercase or lowercase. This prevents errors due to case sensitivity and makes the application more robust. ### Before and After URLs: #### Project Finance - **Before**: `/financial/project/:id:/` - **After**: `/projects/:uuid:/finance/` #### Payments Order Detail - **Before**: `/payments_module/payments/orders//` - **After**: `/payments/orders//` #### Payments Order Retry - **Before**: `/payments_module/payments/orders///retry` - **After**: `/payments/orders///retry` #### Payments Order List - **Before**: `/payments_module/payments/orders/` - **After**: `/payments/orders/` #### Sales Orders - **Before**: `/payments_module/salesorder/` - **After**: `/salesorders/` ### Why These Changes Are Important: 1. **Clarity and Predictability**: A consistent URL structure makes it easier for us to navigate and manage the application. Predictable URLs save us time and reduce errors when developing and debugging. 2. **User Experience**: Case-insensitive URLs prevent user errors and ensure smooth navigation, leading to a better overall experience. 3. **Maintainability**: A well-organized URL structure helps us maintain and scale the application more easily. It'll be simpler to onboard new developers and ensure everyone understands the project's organization. Thank you all for your time and dedication. If you have any feedback or questions about these changes, please feel free to share. Let's work together to keep our project clean and efficient! Best, [Your Name]
summary: Cleaning and organizing inconsistent URL structures in Django project
